Definition of Assistive Technologies
The World Health Organization (WHO) defines assistive technolo-
gies (ATs) as “the application of organized knowledge and skills re-
lated to assistive products, including systems and services.” AT is a 
subset of health technology31 and encompasses a wide range of de-
vices, systems, and tools designed to support disabled individuals in 
performing difficult or impossible daily functions. These technolo-
gies include simple, low-tech tools such as grab bars and magnifying 
glasses to sophisticated, high-tech solutions such as powered wheel-
chairs, communication devices, and exoskeletal robots provided as 
assistive products. According to the International Organization for 
Standardization (ISO) definition, assistive product refers to “any 
product (including devices, equipment, instruments, and software), 
specially produced or generally available, used by or for persons with 
disability for participation; to protect, support, train, measure or 
substitute for body functions/structures and activities; or to prevent 
impairments, activity limitations or participation restrictions.” The 
primary goal of ATs is to enhance the independence, productivity, 
and overall quality of life of individuals facing physical, sensory, or 
cognitive impairments. “Welfare technology” partially overlaps the 
concept of ATs, emphasizing the sociotechnologic aspects and inte-
gration into health care and social care practices.11
Importance of Assistive Technologies  
in Rehabilitation
Functional difficulties are a worldwide problem. In a global sur -
vey, the prevalence of participants reporting functional difficulties 
is seeing (20.9%), mobility (12.1%), cognition (6.4%), hearing 
(4.8%), self-care (4.1%), and communication (2.4%). 76 ATs can 
play a critical role in facilitating recovery and improving patient 
outcomes in both daily living and rehabilitation. The WHO pre-
dicts that by 2030 over 2 billion people will need at least one as -
sistive product. Rehabilitation aims to restore or enhance the 
functional abilities of individuals who have experienced injury, 
illness, or disability. The demand for ATs is significantly higher in 
populations undergoing rehabilitation compared to the general 
population. ATs provide essential support by enabling patients to 
perform everyday tasks, engage in therapeutic exercises, and com-
municate effectively, promoting greater participation in various 
aspects of life. By empowering patients to overcome functional 
limitations and achieve their personal goals, ATs underscore the 
importance of innovation and adaptability in the ongoing efforts 
to improve rehabilitation outcomes. The significance of AT in 
rehabilitation can be highlighted through several key aspects 
(Fig. 20.1 ).
Enhanced Mobility and Independence
Mobility aids such as wheelchairs, prosthetics, and walkers enable 
individuals to move freely and independently, reducing reliance 
on caregivers and enhancing the ability to perform daily activities.
Improved Communication
For individuals with speech or hearing impairments, communica-
tion aids such as speech-generating devices and hearing aids facilitate 
• AT development must be personalized, focusing on individual 
needs, functionality, and adaptability.
• Barriers to AT include cost, complexity, and lack of awareness. 
Multidisciplinary collaboration and evidence-based practices 
are essential.
• Integration of artificial intelligence, wearable technologies, and 
3D printing will further enhance the effectiveness and 
 accessibility of AT.
20
Assistive Technology and Environmental 
Control Devices
SHIH-CHING CHEN AND JIUNN-HORNG KANG
• Assistive technology (AT) encompasses devices, systems, and 
tools that support individuals with disabilities in performing 
tasks that may otherwise be difficult or impossible.
• AT facilitates recovery and improves patient outcomes by 
 enabling functional independence in daily living activities.
• AT has progressed from simple aids such as crutches in  
ancient times to modern innovations such as robotic  
exoskeletons.
SUMMARY PEARLS

emotional well-being.
Support for Cognitive Functioning
Cognitive aids, including memory aids and specialized software, assist 
individuals with cognitive impairments in managing daily tasks, im-
proving organization, and enhancing overall cognitive functioning.
Facilitation of Daily Living Activities
Devices designed to assist with activities of daily living, such as 
adaptive utensils and dressing aids, enable individuals to perform 
self-care tasks more efficiently, fostering a sense of autonomy and 
self-esteem.
Promotion of Participation
ATs help bridge the gap between individuals with disabilities and 
their environments, promoting greater participation in educational, 
vocational, and recreational activities. This inclusion is essential for 
the holistic rehabilitation of individuals, ensuring they can lead ful-
filling and active lives. Rehabilitation professionals should be trained 
to assess and recommend suitable ATs for each case. Rehabilitation 
programs should integrate state-of-the-art ATs and remain actively 
engaged in research to address identified gaps and challenges.
Evolution and Milestones of Assistive 
Technologies
The history of AT is marked by a continuous evolution driven by 
human ingenuity and the desire to improve the quality of life for 
individuals with disabilities. In ancient Egypt, simple wooden 
crutches were used to aid mobility, while the Greeks and Romans 
designed early prosthetic limbs made from wood and metal. 
 During the Middle Ages, there were innovations such as hand-
operated carts for mobility and magnifying glasses to aid vision. 
The Industrial Revolution in the 18th and 19th centuries marked 
a significant turning point in the development of ATs. Engineering 
and materials science advances have led to the creation of more 
sophisticated and effective devices. The 20th century saw rapid

significantly impacting the field of AT . Following both world wars, 
there was a surge in the development of prosthetics and orthotics 
caused by the increased number of injured veterans. This period 
also saw the introduction of powered wheelchairs and the initial 
development of electronic aids for those who were visually and 
hearing impaired. Table 20.1 summarizes the key milestones in 
modern AT development.
From ancient rudimentary aids to today’s sophisticated digital 
devices, each milestone has contributed to the empowerment and 
independence of individuals with disabilities. As technology ad -
vances, the potential for further innovations in AT remains vast, 
promising even greater enhancements in the quality of life and 
rehabilitation outcomes for those in need.
Principles in Designing and Delivering 
Assistive Technologies
Designing and delivering ATs require a comprehensive under -
standing of the users’ needs, abilities, and environment. Effec-
tive AT should be user centered, functional, and adaptable to 
ensure it provides meaningful benefits. The framework for de -
veloping metrics to evaluate the ergonomic features of ATs has 
been proposed and summarized.
74 Several essential frameworks 
have been proposed that consider the use of scenario and hu -
man-technology-environment-social interactions ( Table 20.2). 
The International Classification of Functioning, Disability, and 
Health (ICF) was the most frequently used framework. This 
framework helps classify the health and disability components 
of functioning and contextual factors, emphasizing the role of 
environmental and personal factors in the effectiveness of ATs. 
The human activity AT is another framework that describes the 
interaction between human activity, AT , and the contextual en -
vironment, highlighting how personal and environmental fac-
tors can enhance or restrict the use of AT .
5
The design and delivery process of ATs should involve an interdis-
ciplinary team, including health care professionals, engineers, thera-
pists, and users, to ensure a holistic approach. Several essential steps 
in the AT service and delivery process have been recommended: 
 assessment, formulation of a care plan, selection, fitting, training, 
follow-up, and evaluation ( Table 20.3). The AT service delivery

process is attracting interest. A systematic literature review showed 
that factors in almost all the process steps affect satisfaction with the 
devices, which can lead to underutilization and abandonment of ATs.  
A client-centered approach to the process is advocated.
42 Providing 
adequate training and support to users and their caregivers is crucial 
to use ATs effectively. Continuous support and maintenance services 
should be available to address any issues that arise while using the 
technology. Finally, the comprehensive process of AT service delivery 
should be supported by clinical evidence. The main aspects in design-
ing and delivering ATs should be considered (Fig. 20.2).
User-Centered Design
ATs must be tailored to the specific needs and preferences of indi-
vidual users. This includes considering their physical abilities, 
cognitive capacities, and personal goals. Users should be actively 
involved in the design and selection process to ensure the technol-
ogy meets their needs and fits into their daily lives. The technol -
ogy should be intuitive and easy to operate, minimizing the 
 learning curve and promoting user independence. Ergonomic 
principles of ATs have been addressed in specific standards (e.g., 
ISO 9241-110:2006 or DIN 92419) to enhance the quality of the 
human technology interaction with ATs.
74 User feedback and 
preference are crucial in shaping AT systems to meet users’ real-
world needs and preferences. For example, a review demonstrates 
that individuals who are blind and visually impaired may be 
 willing to sacrifice features for more reliable navigation while 
considering usability and user experience. 69 The role of formal 
and informal caregivers in the context of AT should be noted. 
Nevertheless, their needs and interactions with technology are 
often underexplored. Caregivers frequently face the physical and 
emotional burdens of their work. They use, adapt, and shape ATs 
in their caregiving roles, pointing to the need for designs that 
consider the caregivers’ work environments and challenges.
9
Functionality and Effectiveness
The functionality of the technology in achieving users’ specific goals 
is related to their mobility, communication, or daily living activi-
ties. Human factor and ergonomic considerations are considered in 
the design and development process of ATs, aiming to emphasize 
users’ unique requirements.
3 The technology must be reliable and 
durable to withstand regular use and provide consistent support to 
each user. Regular assessment of the technology’s performance is 
essential to ensure it continues to meet users’ needs and to make 
necessary adjustments.

Accessibility, Affordability, and Adaptability
ATs should be designed using universal design principles, ensur -
ing accessibility and usability by the widest possible range of 
 users. The technology should be intuitive and easy to use, mini -
mizing the learning curve and reducing the likelihood of user 
frustration. The AT should be affordable for users, considering 
their financial situation and potential funding sources, such as 
insurance or grants. The design should comply with accessibility 
standards to ensure it is easily used by individuals with various 
types of disabilities. The technology should be readily available 
and accessible to obtain, with consideration given to geographic 
and socioeconomic factors. ATs should be adaptable to the users’ 
changing needs and environmental factors. This includes modular 
designs that allow for upgrades and modifications as needed. The 
technology should also be compatible with other devices and 
systems that users may already be utilizing, thus ensuring seamless 
integration into their daily routines.
Safety and Comfort
Potential risks and harms associated with using the technology 
should be thoroughly assessed and minimized. The design should 
prioritize user comfort and ergonomics to prevent strain or injury 
from prolonged use. The AT must meet safety standards to avoid 
accidents or harm to each user. Prothesis and orthosis can cause 
skin irritation and pain caused by improper fitting issues. Provi -
sion for regular maintenance and access to technical support are 
crucial to ensure the technology remains safe and functional over 
time. The safety of ATs is highly dependent on the environment 
in which they are utilized. For example, wheelchair users may 
encounter difficulties navigating uneven terrain, leading to acci -
dents. Sideways tipping and forward falls from wheelchairs are 
highly influenced by curb height and the approach angle. Using 
seat belts can mitigate the risk of falls and reduce head impact 
forces during sideways tipping incidents.
27 It should be empha-
sized that some adverse events associated with ATs could be 
 overlooked and are not limited to physical conditions. It has been 
reported that some technologies may lead to increased anxiety, 
agitation, or decreased interaction with caregivers, pointing to the 
need for careful consideration of technology design and its 
implementation in dementia care.
29 It has been proposed that 
changes in the risk tolerance of children and their parents after the 
introduction of ATs or protective equipment for children exist; it 
is possible AT devices could interact with other known risk factors 
to promote injury risk-taking behaviors in children.
57
Outcome Measurements, Impacts,  
and Evidence-Based Practice
The benefits of using ATs could be multidimensional, including 
physical, psychological, social, and well-being. Outcome measure-
ments using ATs are fundamental for advancing the field, optimiz-
ing individual user experiences, and ensuring efficient use of health 
care resources. The outcome measurements for ATs are generally 
categorized into four main types: functional efficacy, satisfaction, 
psychosocial impacts, and quality of life.
12 Table 20.4 demon-
strates some common outcome measurements related to AT 
service. A system of practice that incorporates a combination of 
validated tools and measures to address various purposes and per-
spectives is likely to be beneficial.
59 Combining these domains in 
future AT evaluation may provide holistic views of AT utiliza -
tion.68 A relationship has been recognized between the psychoso -
cial impact and the probability of future abandonment of certain 
ATs.
38 The data bridging the gap between clinical expectations and 
real-world user experiences foster a more responsive and  r esponsible 
approach to AT deployment. It can improve user satisfaction and 
quality of life, guide device customization and innovation, en -
hance clinical decision making, justify funding and insurance 
coverage, and facilitate policy development and implementation. 
Several outcome measurements have been developed to demon-
strate the reliability, validity, and utility (e.g., Quebec User Evalu-
ation of Satisfaction with Assistive T echnology [QUEST 2.0]).
6 
Nevertheless, using these measurements should still be reverified 
for different social and cultural backgrounds. Some areas, such as 
mobility, have numerous studies, whereas other domains, such as 
ATs for domestic activities and communication, are less studied.
12
Using adequate ATs can also improve clinical outcomes for 
specific populations. For example, a study reported that patients 
with personal hearing amplifiers in emergency departments and 
acute care settings showed improved engagement of medical 
instructions and were less likely to revisit the emergency depart -
ment within 30 days.
13 Access to suitable wheelchairs with proper 
cushions significantly reduces the frequency of pressure sores or 
urinary tract infections.

falls in older adults living at home, which has been reported. 58
ATs significantly enhance educational engagement and psy -
chosocial well-being for students with various disabilities within 
higher education institutions.
50 When effectively implemented 
in work environments, ATs can improve employee productivity.62 
Therefore ATs may reduce the risk of joblessness and poverty 
often experienced by people with functional difficulties. The 
provision of ATs involves a thoughtful and comprehensive ap -
proach that prioritizes users’ needs and preferences. By adhering 
to these principles, designers and health care providers can en-
sure that ATs are effective, accessible, and beneficial to those 
who rely on them. This user-centered focus not only enhances 
the quality of life for people with disabilities but also promotes 
greater inclusion and participation in all aspects of society. 
 Because of the high heterogeneity of disabilities and the diffi -
culty of conducting sham control trials in the AT field, high-
quality evidence to determine AT effectiveness remains relatively 
scanty. In addition, more comparative and longitudinal studies 
of ATs and research involving diverse groups within and across 
countries should be emphasized. Therefore enhancing data col -
lection methods in the real-world setting by integrating more 
 specific questions about AT into existing health and disability 
surveys will improve the quality and comparability of data 
across studies. Updating and maintaining large international 
data repositories like the AT2030 data portal is vital to support 
research and policymaking by providing a source of up-to-date 
information on AT access.
8 This allows stakeholders, including 
researchers, policymakers, and advocacy groups, to identify gaps 
in AT provision.
21
Classifications of Assistive Technologies
The AT classification system is complex and can be categorized 
based on functionality, type of assistance provided, or specific 
needs they address. The classification systems for ATs are sum -
marized in Table 20.5. The WHO provides the ICF classification 
system, which is a framework that classifies AT based on how it 
supports participation and activity, considering factors such as 
body functions and structures, activities and participation, and 
environmental factors. Following is an overview of the main tax-
onomy and classifications within AT .
Mobility Aids
Mobility aids are designed to assist individuals with mobility 
impairments to move around or manipulate objects more effi -
ciently and safely. Sophisticated devices are designed according 
to specific needs. Modern wearable robotic suits assist individu -
als with severe mobility impairments in walking. Exoskeletons 
have shown promise in both rehabilitation settings and for daily 
use, enabling users to stand and walk with greater stability. 
Modern wheelchairs are equipped with intelligent technology, 
such as GPS, collision detection, and self-balancing systems, 
which enhance users’ mobility and safety. Mobile arm support is 
designed to assist with upper extremity motor skills, compensat -
ing for muscle weakness to perform diverse activities such as 
opening a container and handwriting. Examples include the 
following:
Walking aids: Canes, quad canes, and crutches provide basic 
support and balance for walking.

Wheelchairs: Manual and powered wheelchairs (Video 20.1) provide 
mobility for individuals who cannot walk.
Scooters: Electric scooters offer an alternative to wheelchairs for 
those with limited walking ability.
Walkers and rollators: These provide support and stability for indi-
viduals who can walk but need assistance. They are equipped 
with wheels and often come with seats and brakes, allowing 
easier movement over smoother surfaces. Some power wheel-
chairs can even overcome obstacles and environmental barri -
ers, such as step-climbing power wheelchairs.
67
Orthoses: Orthoses are devices used to support, align, prevent, or 
correct deformities or to improve the body’s function. They 
can be custom made or prefabricated and designed to fit spe -
cific body parts, such as the limbs or trunk (Fig. 20.3).
Prosthetics: Artificial limbs that replace missing body parts, en -
hancing mobility and function.
Exoskeletons and robotic devices:  Exoskeletal devices work in con -
junction with the user’s body. Depending on the design and 
intended use, they can be powered by motors and actuators or 
purely mechanical (passive) (Fig. 20.4).
Communication Aids
Communication aids, or augmentative and alternative communi-
cation (AAC) devices, help individuals with speech or language 
impairments communicate more effectively. Modern AAC devices 
incorporate advanced features such as predictive text, voice synthe-
sis, and customizable interfaces. These devices are also becoming 
more portable and affordable. Improved algorithms and machine 
learning techniques have made speech recognition software more 
accurate and responsive, allowing users to control devices and 
communicate more effectively. Examples include the following:
Speech-generating devices: Electronic devices that produce spoken 
words when the user selects symbols or types of text.
Communication boards: Boards with pictures, symbols, or words 
that individuals can point to in order to communicate. Pro-
grams or apps functionalized with iPad, iPhone, Windows, or 
Android systems are available (Fig. 20.5).
T ext-to-speech software: Software that converts written text into 
spoken words, useful for individuals with speech impairments.
• Fig. 20.3 A dynamic hand orthosis that trains patients’ hand function by

Cognitive Aids
Cognitive aids are tools designed to support individuals with 
cognitive impairments in managing daily tasks, improving cogni-
tive function, and compensating for difficulties in memory, atten-
tion, problem solving, and other cognitive functions (Fig. 20.6). 
Examples include the following:
Memory aids: Devices or apps that help individuals remember 
important tasks, appointments, or information (e.g., electronic 
organizers, reminder apps).
Specialized software: Programs to enhance cognitive skills such as 
attention, memory, and problem solving (e.g., brain training 
apps, educational software).
Electronic calendars: Digital calendars can be set with reminders 
and alerts to help manage schedules and tasks.
Sensory Aids
Sensory aids assist individuals with sensory impairments, such as 
vision or hearing loss, by enhancing their ability to perceive their 
environment. Advanced hearing aids have Bluetooth connectivity, 
noise cancellation, and directional microphones, providing a 
 better listening experience. Some models also integrate with 
smartphones, allowing for remote adjustments and settings cus -
tomization. Haptic ATs utilize touch and tactile feedback to assist 
individuals with hearing or visual impairments. These technolo -
gies convert auditory or visual information into tactile sensations, 
enabling users to perceive and interact with their environment 
more effectively.
63 T echnologic advancements have led to the de-
velopment of sophisticated vision aids, such as smart glasses that 
use augmented reality to enhance visual perception and electronic 
implants that can partially restore vision for individuals with cer -
tain types of blindness. Examples include the following:
Hearing aids and personal hearing amplifiers (PHAs):  Devices that 
amplify sound for individuals with hearing loss. Hearing aids 
are custom-fit devices to compensate for specific hearing defi -
cits. PHAs are generally over-the-counter products that am -
plify sounds to make them louder and clearer.
Cochlear implants: Surgically implanted devices that provide a 
sense of sound to individuals with severe hearing loss.
Vision aids: T ools such as spectacles, magnifiers, screen readers, 
and braille displays that assist individuals with visual impair -
ments depending on their severity and types ( Video 20.2).
Navigation systems: Systems that are tailored for individuals with 
visual impairment, spanning traditional tools such as the 
white cane to modern technologic solutions such as elec -
tronic devices and software applications. These solutions are 
categorized into visual imagery, nonvisual data, map-based 
systems, and 3D sound systems. These technologies contrib -
ute to obstacle detection and route optimization and provide 
real-time feedback, thereby improving independence and 
safety for users.
2
Alerting devices: Devices that use visual or tactile alerts to signal 
important events, such as doorbells, alarms, or phone calls, for 
individuals with hearing loss.
Medications assistive devices: Individuals with sensory impairment 
could have an increased risk of medication-related iatrogenic 
disease. Specialized ATs could support medication manage -
ment for individuals with sensory impairment to reduce harm. 
The range of technologies and devices includes applications for 
Android phones, audio-prescription labeling/reading systems, 
touch-to-speech devices, and magnifying technology.

Daily living aids are tools designed to help individuals perform 
everyday activities more efficiently and independently. Examples 
include the following:
Adaptive utensils: Modified eating utensils with ergonomic han-
dles or specialized grips to assist individuals with limited hand 
function.
Dressing aids: T ools such as button hooks, zipper pulls, and dress-
ing sticks that help individuals dress independently.

Things devices into assistive devices allows for seamless control of 
home environments. Intelligent lights, thermostats, and appliances 
can be controlled via voice commands, mobile apps, or adaptive 
switches, improving accessibility and convenience. Modern envi-
ronment control systems are built on various wireless systems, each 
offering unique ways to manage and control a user’s environment. 
Infrared control systems, Bluetooth systems, and Wi-Fi are popu-
lar choices for home automation. These wireless systems are also 
helpful for individuals who require remote access to their devices. 
Except for wireless systems, the advancement of user interface 
systems makes environmental control user friendly. Devices such 
as Amazon Echo and Google Home have become central hubs for 
AT , enabling users to perform various tasks through voice com -
mands, from setting reminders to controlling household devices. 
This technology can control various aspects of a home environ -
ment, including doors, blinds, heating and cooling systems, light-
ing, windows, doorbells, intercom systems, taps, showers, and 
 entertainment systems through smartphones, tablets, and voice-
controlled systems. The primary aim of home automation is to 
increase independence and enhance the quality of life for individu-
als, particularly those with physical, sensory, or intellectual 
 disabilities.15 Home automation also facilitates greater social inter-
action and community engagement by enabling individuals to 
communicate and participate in social activities more easily. 
 Improved self-esteem, reduced anxiety, and better psychological 
well-being among users have been shown in disabled patients using 
home automation to enhance independence and autonomy.
15 The 
participants experience reduced social isolation and increased in-
teraction with friends and family through smart devices for com -
munication.
15 Examples include the following:
Voice-controlled devices: Smart home devices such as Amazon Echo 
or Google Home that can control lights, thermostats, and 
other appliances through voice commands.
Switch-activated controls: Systems allow users to operate devices 
using simple switches that can be activated by various body 
movements.
Remote controls: Devices that enable users to control electronic 
appliances and systems from a distance.
Computer Access Aids
Computer access aids are tools and devices that enable individuals 
with disabilities to use computers and mobile devices more

 Examples include the following:
Alternative keyboards:  Keyboards with different layouts, larger 
keys, or customizable settings to accommodate various physi -

Trackballs and joysticks: Pointing devices that offer alternative

Eye-tracking device: A tool for controlling a computer or other

Screen readers: Software reads aloud the content displayed on a

Speech recognition software: Programs convert spoken words into 
text, allowing users to control computers and write documents 
using their voice.
Computer access software: Programs that allow users to directly ac-
cess other accessibility/usability features on computers, such as 
text size, magnifier, and read selection.
Educational Aids
Educational aids are tools designed to support learning and 
academic performance for individuals with disabilities. These 
aids can be physical devices, digital tools, or instructional ma -
terials that help facilitate the acquisition of knowledge and 
skills. They are particularly beneficial in providing personalized 
learning experiences, accommodating diverse learning styles, 
and addressing specific educational needs. Examples include 
the following:
Adaptive learning software: Programs that adjust the difficulty level 
based on the user’s performance and needs.
Audiobooks and ebooks: Accessible formats of books that can be 
read aloud by a computer or other device.
Specialized educational apps: These applications support various 
learning styles and needs, such as visual aids, interactive activi-
ties, and customized exercises.
Speech-to-text technology: Software or system that converts spoken 
language into written text. This technology is useful for creat -
ing documents, sending messages, or performing any other 
activity that typically requires typing.

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Caregiver Aids
ATs, such as grab bars, sensor systems, and companion robots, are 
examined for their potential to alleviate some physical and psy -
chological stresses on caregivers. For example, sensor and moni -
toring systems can enhance the safety and coordination of care in 
care homes by providing alerts and monitoring residents’ activi -
ties, thus reducing constant physical supervision needs and allow-
ing caregivers to manage their tasks more effectively.
14 The slide 
boards used by home care aides to assist client transfers highlight 
proper equipment that can significantly reduce biomechanical 
loading on caregivers, thereby potentially reducing injuries and 
physical strain.
14 Examples include the following:
Transfer assistive devices: These devices, such as sliding boards, lift-
ers, raisers, and supporting belts, help caregivers transfer pa-
tients effectively and safely (Fig. 20.7).
Real-time locating systems: These systems automatically identify 
and track the location of objects or people in real time, typi -
cally within a building or other contained area. They can 
 potentially improve residents’ safety, quality of care, and effi -
ciency of long-term care facilities.33
Assistive Technology Needs and 
Considerations for Specific Populations
Older Adults
Aging is a complicated, dynamic, and individualized process. 
 Accelerating population aging is a global issue. How to achieve suc-
cessful and healthy aging and the rising demand for aging-in-place 
technologies have drawn increasing attention. 55 Living indepen -
dently can be challenging for seniors. Furthermore, the global chal-
lenge of an aging population outpacing the availability of caregivers 
necessitates technologies that enable older adults to live indepen -
dently at home.
64 Cochrane reviews demonstrated that home fall-
hazard interventions, including ATs, could reduce the overall rate of 
falls by 26%; however, these interventions were more effective in 
people with a higher risk of falling.
16 Among people who fall at 
home, seniors without using walking aids could sustain more severe 
injuries.
48 On the other hand, the prevalence of chronic diseases is 
significantly higher in the geriatric population compared to the 
general population. It is noteworthy that aging people with 
chronic diseases may also require technologies tailored to manag -
ing specific symptoms or conditions, adding to the complexity. For 
instance, those with diabetes might benefit from devices that 
monitor blood glucose levels and alert users to take insulin. 
 Walking aids may reduce functional recline in sarcopenic pa -
tients.61 Given that these chronic conditions can come with 
physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments, the user interface of 
ATs needs to be particularly intuitive and easy to use for those with 
specific disabilities (Fig. 20.8). Furthermore, there is often a higher 
need for ATs to integrate seamlessly with other health care systems 
to ensure continuous care. This includes the ability to share data 
with health care providers easily. T echnologies for the general older 
population may focus more on enhancing daily living than inte -
grating closely with medical care.
10
Children
Pediatric populations using ATs have specific characteristics to 
consider when designing, prescribing, and managing ATs.
28 
 Children grow rapidly, so their assistive devices may need fre -
quent adjustments, resizing, and updates to accommodate their 
physical growth and changes in their abilities and needs. ATs 
must support and not hinder the achievement of developmental 
milestones, such as motor skills, social skills, and language devel -
opment. Furthermore, it has been noted that the widespread lack 
of ATs for children with disabilities leads to lower primary school 
completion rates. Education needs should be considered explic -
itly in children with disability.
53 Incorporating ATs promotes 
educational engagement and learning efficacy, which is para -
mount in care. Devices must be user friendly and suitable for 
children, often incorporating engaging and appealing elements 
to younger users. This can include using bright colors, interactive 
features, and designs that appeal to their imagination and inter-
ests. Family members and caregivers play a critical role in success-
fully utilizing ATs. They also need proper training and support to 
integrate these technologies into a child’s daily routine. Emo -
tional and practical support for families is essential because man -
aging assistive devices can add to the caregiving burden.
54 The 
family-centered model could be helpful for AT provision for the 
pediatric population.

Future Directions of Assistive Technology
The field of ATs has seen significant advancements in recent years, 
driven by rapid developments in engineering, materials science, 
and digital technology.
1 These innovations have greatly expanded 
the capabilities of assistive devices, making them more effective, 
accessible, and user friendly. As technologies continue to evolve, 
the future of AT holds exciting possibilities that promise to en -
hance the lives of individuals with disabilities further. This section 
highlights key areas of development.
Increased Personalization and Customization
Three-dimensional scanning helps with personalized customiza-
tion and optimizes comfort and functionality for individual users. 
Three-dimensional printing technology will create highly custom-
ized assistive devices that fit the user’s unique anatomy and 
needs.
72 This can include custom prosthetics, orthotics, and adap-
tive tools. Machine learning algorithms that adapt in real time to 
the user’s behavior and preferences will make assistive devices 
more intuitive and effective. Researchers are exploring the use of 
intelligent materials that can adapt to environmental changes, 
providing more responsive and adaptable solutions for users.
39,44
Integration of New Sensors With Wearable 
Technologies and Health Data in Assistive 
Technologies
Advanced sensors have evolved to be multifunctional, highly 
 integrated, and compact. 44 They can be mounted on clothing, 
 devices, or bodies to continuously monitor physiologic signals and 
enhance interaction and communication with the environment and 
equipment.32,39 Wearable devices increasingly incorporate health 
monitoring features, such as tracking vital signs,  detecting falls, and 
alerting caregivers in case of emergencies. This integration will pro-
vide users with continuous health monitoring and proactive health 
care management. Wearable technology can also help track physical 
activity, provide feedback, support rehabilitation efforts, and 
 promote an active lifestyle. Artificial intelligence (AI)–powered 
 applications are increasingly used to create personalized learning 
experiences and cognitive training programs. These tools adapt to 
the user’s progress and provide tailored exercises to improve cogni-
tive functions. Wearable devices and smartphone apps that use AI 
help individuals with memory impairments by giving reminders, 
tracking daily activities, and organizing schedules.
Advanced Robotics and New Artificial 
Intelligence Models
Robots capable of performing a wide range of tasks, from fetching 
items to providing companionship, will become more common. 
These robots will significantly benefit individuals with severe mo-
bility impairments or cognitive disabilities.
4 AI-powered virtual 
companions will offer emotional support, cognitive stimulation, 
and assistance with daily tasks, enhancing users’ overall quality of 
life. Generative AI (GAI) refers to a subset of AI technologies ca -
pable of generating new content that resembles existing data. This 
can include text, images, audio, and other forms of media or data. 
The key attribute of GAI is its ability to learn from a set of data 
and then use that learned information to create new data instances.

GAI enhances existing components of the brain-  computer inter-

26
Virtual Reality in Assistive Technologies
Virtual reality (VR) is a computer-generated simulation of an 
environment that allows users to interact with it in a seemingly 
natural or physical way through special electronic equipment, 
such as headsets with screens or gloves fitted with sensors. VR can 
be categorized into different types, from nonimmersive VR to 
augmented reality and mixed reality. VR becomes one of the valu-
able mediums for delivering therapy by creating an immersive 
environment that allows users to engage in scenarios that feel real, 
which is particularly beneficial for therapeutic and educational 
applications. VR environments are highly interactive, safe, and 
well controlled. VR systems can be extensively customized to 
meet the specific needs of individuals with different disabilities. 
This adaptability ensures that VR can assist a wide range of dis -
abilities, from motor and cognitive impairments to sensory and 
communication disorders.
41,65 The users can practice and learn 
new skills without the real-world consequences of failure 
(Fig.  20.9). This is particularly important for users with severe 
impairments or those recovering from trauma or disease.
Barriers and Challenges in Using Assistive 
Technologies
The underadoption of ATs has been recognized in real-world cir-
cumstances. A survey of the adoption rates that included 22,666 
care-dependent persons and caregivers reports that sophisticated 
types of ATs are still rather scarcely used for home care arrange -
ments.37 Although ATs offer significant benefits to individuals 
with disabilities, several barriers and challenges can hinder their 
practical use. These obstacles can stem from various sources, in-
cluding economic, technical, social, and personal factors. There 
are multiple barriers across different domains, such as cost, com -
plexity of interventions, and lack of evidence of effectiveness 
(Fig. 20.10). For instance, product-related factors such as com-
fort, durability, fit, and limited choices were significant barriers.
5 
This discrepancy between users’ needs and the system’s provision 
can result in abandonment, especially when the users need to 
adapt to the technology (technology centered).
36 Understanding 
these challenges is crucial for developing strategies to overcome 
them and ensure that ATs can reach their full potential in improv-
ing users’ lives.
49 Many patients face significant delays or outright 
denials when receiving these technologies. This lack of timely ac -
cess can adversely impact their recovery. The study identified that 
only 55% of the assistive devices were delivered to children, with 
delivery times ranging from 12 to 250 days, showcasing consider-
able variability and potential for delayed rehabilitation.
28 Another 
example is a gap in the availability of modern braille technology, 
such as refreshable braille displays, especially in less affluent 
 regions or schools with limited funding. This disparity limits 
 students’ exposure to braille, affecting their literacy and overall 
academic performance.
Economic Barriers
Over 2.5 billion people currently require one or more assistive 
products, which is expected to rise to 3.5 billion by 2050. Access

disparities are significant, with only about 3% of those in low-

43 Many assis-
tive devices are expensive, making them inaccessible to individuals 
with limited financial resources. The high development, custom -
ization, and maintenance costs can also be prohibitive.  I nsufficient 
funding and reimbursement options from insurance companies 
and government programs can limit users’ ability to afford neces-
sary ATs.
Technical Barriers
Some ATs are complex and difficult to use, especially for indi -
viduals with limited technical skills. Poor usability can deter users 
from adopting these devices. Adequate user interface design, 
known as human-technology interface (HTI), should be tailored 
to target users. HTI consists of different kinds of interaction, in-
cluding brain-computer interface, human-robot interaction, and 
speech-, gesture-, tongue-, eye-, and multimodal-based interac -
tions.
74 As more ATs and systems are introduced and launched, 
interoperability between them should be carefully evaluated. 
 Assistive devices may not always be compatible with other tech -
nologies or systems, leading to difficulties in integration and 
functionality. The need for regular maintenance and the unavail -
ability of repair services can be significant challenges, especially in 
remote or underserved areas.
Social and Cultural Barriers
The use of ATs can sometimes carry a social stigma, leading to re-
luctance or embarrassment in using these devices publicly. This can 
affect the user’s willingness to adopt and consistently use ATs. 
Cultural attitudes toward disability and technology can influence 
the acceptance and use of assistive devices. In some cultures, there 
may be resistance to adopting new technologies. Potential users, 
caregivers, and health care providers often lack awareness of the 
available ATs and their benefits, particularly in this field, which 
advances and evolves quickly. T echnology should empower users 
by enhancing their capabilities and promoting independence 
rather than creating dependence or stigmatization. Strategies to 
increase technology acceptance among the population might in -
clude targeted education to improve digital literacy and designing 
user-friendly technology solutions. Insufficient training for users 
and their support networks can result in improper use or underuti-
lization of assistive devices. T raining is essential for maximizing the 
effectiveness of these technologies. The integration of AT provision 
training within the curricula of various professional rehabilitation 
organizations should be highlighted. There is an emphasis on the 
need for educational programs to include and expand upon this 
area to ensure entry-level practitioners are competent. Another 
example is a notable deficiency in braille-trained teachers and the 
inclusion of braille in the curriculum. This lack of expertise leads 
to insufficient support for braille as a medium of instruction, im-
pacting students’ ability to learn effectively.
Environmental and Contextual Barriers
The physical environment, including inaccessible buildings, trans-
portation, and public spaces, can limit the effectiveness and use of 
ATs. Limited access to the internet and digital services in some 
regions can prevent individuals from utilizing modern ATs that 
rely on connectivity and digital platforms. Workplace accommo -
dation is an important factor in supporting individuals with dis -
abilities in using specialized equipment at work.

Policy and Regulatory Barriers
Developers and manufacturers must navigate the complexities of 
intellectual property law to protect their innovations while re -
specting the rights of others. On the other hand, consideration 
should be given to the benefits of open-source technologies and 
shared innovation to promote wider access and collaboration. 
Lengthy and complex regulatory approval processes can delay the 
availability of new or innovative ATs. Navigating these regulations 
can also be challenging for developers and manufacturers. 
 Furthermore, inconsistent standards and regulations across differ-
ent regions can create barriers to the distribution and use of ATs. 
Inadequate policies and government support can limit access to 
ATs. Policies are needed to ensure funding, support services, and 
incentives for developing and distributing these devices.
Personal Barriers
Individual preferences and comfort levels with technology can 
vary significantly, affecting the adoption and consistent use of as-
sistive devices. The outlook and visibility of assistive devices also 
affect the acceptance and self-agency of people with disabilities. 46 
Some individuals may have physical or cognitive limitations that 
make it difficult to use certain types of ATs effectively. Resistance 
to change and adaptation to new technologies can be a significant 
barrier.20 Users may feel overwhelmed or intimidated by the in -
troduction of new devices. 75 Worth noting, studies showed that 
participants with substantial or severe fragility showed no effec -
tiveness in improving quality of life by ATs addressing age-related 
difficulties.30 Clinicians should be aware of the subgroup during 
the provision of ATs. The barriers and challenges in using ATs are 
multifaceted and interrelated. Addressing these obstacles requires 
a comprehensive approach that includes improving affordability, 
enhancing usability, raising awareness, and fostering supportive 
policies. By tackling these challenges, stakeholders can work to -
ward making AT more accessible and effective for individuals 
with disabilities.
Ethical and Legal Considerations of  
Assistive Technologies
The use of ATs raises various ethical and legal considerations that 
must be addressed to ensure that these technologies are developed, 
implemented, and used to respect the rights and dignity of indi -
viduals with disabilities. These considerations include privacy, 
autonomy, equity, and regulatory compliance.
Privacy and Confidentiality
With the integration of sensors, ATs may collect and store sensi -
tive personal information, including health data, location, and 
usage patterns. Ensuring the data are securely stored and transmit-
ted is paramount to protecting user privacy. Users must be fully 
informed about what data are being collected, how the data will 
be used, and who will have access to the data. Obtaining informed 
consent is essential to maintaining trust and transparency. Policies 
about data ownership need to be defined. The design and use of 
ATs must respect user privacy and ensure the confidentiality of 
personal and medical information. Users should be fully informed 
about the AT’s benefits, risks, and limitations and provide consent 
before implementation. With specific video-based technologies 
adopted, privacy-preserving solutions should be considered 
 thoroughly.52 Caregivers and caring staff are susceptible to the 
possibility that technologies could be used for performance man -
agement.34 How extensive data collected by monitoring technolo-
gies could constitute evidence of appropriate care delivery should 
be further investigated.
Autonomy and Independence
AT should be designed to empower users by enhancing their in -
dependence and enabling them to make their own decisions. 
T echnologies should allow for customization to meet users’ spe-
cific needs and preferences, providing them with control over how 
the device functions. Users should be provided with clear and 
comprehensive information about the available ATs, allowing 
them to make informed choices that best suit their needs.
Equity and Fairness
The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities serves 
as a fundamental document guiding countries to adopt AT poli -
cies that promote accessibility and participation in society. 35 All 
individuals, regardless of their disability, should have equal access 
to ATs. Policies and practices should be in place to prevent dis -
crimination and ensure equitable distribution. The development 
and use of ATs should respect the inherent dignity and human 
rights of individuals with disabilities. Efforts should be made to 
ensure that using ATs does not lead to stigmatization or margin -
alization of users. Efforts should be made to reduce the cost of 
ATs, making them affordable and accessible to individuals from 
all socioeconomic backgrounds. Government programs, insur -
ance coverage, and other funding mechanisms should be in place 
to help offset the costs of assistive devices.
Legal and Regulatory Compliance
Compliance emphasizes that AT policies should be grounded in a 
human rights framework, ensuring that individuals with disabili -
ties can access necessary technologies. It critiques the market-
driven approach that views people with disabilities as consumers 
rather than rights holders.25 ATs must comply with relevant laws 
and regulations, including safety, efficacy, and accessibility. Adher-
ence to established standards (e.g., ISO, Americans With 
 Disabilities Act of 1990) ensures that devices meet minimum 
performance and safety requirements. Ethical and legal consider -
ations are integral to the responsible development and use of AT . 
By addressing issues related to privacy, autonomy, accessibility, 
equity, and regulatory compliance, stakeholders can ensure that 
ATs are both effective and respectful of the rights and dignity of 
individuals with disabilities. This approach enhances the impact of 
these technologies and promotes a more inclusive and equitable 
society. Finally, governments can leverage their power to enforce 
accessibility standards and stimulate the market for AT products.25 
Implementing comprehensive national AT plans ensures consis -
tent access to AT across different regions and populations. These 
plans should involve multiple sectors, including health, education, 
and social welfare.25 In recent years, sustainability has gained more 
attention in all fields. The Systemic Development Model, devel -
oped by the World Engagement Institute, addresses four intercon-
nected pillars of sustainability: health, culture, economics, and 
politics. This model aims to improve understanding of personal, 
organizational, and institutional capacities.5 It  encourages a  holistic

Physician’s Role in Health Care Team in 
Assistive Technology Usage
In the field of AT , interdisciplinary collaboration is essential 
whether through interdisciplinary team models or transdisci -
plinary approaches. The delivery process for ATs could vary in 
different countries and societies. Not every type of AT requires 
assessment by medical personnel. Simple aids or devices, such as 
canes or knee braces, can be evaluated and delivered by trained 
personnel. However, for more complex devices or those with po -
tential usage risks, it is essential for trained medical professionals 
rather than venders to conduct the evaluation and prescription. 
Particularly when users have complex or severe conditions, a com-
prehensive assessment and recommendation by a physician may 
be necessary to ensure the effectiveness of the devices in the con -
text of the illness. Physicians’ pivotal roles involve confirming 
patients’ information of diagnoses and prognoses, gathering the 
demographic and environmental data of patients, performing 
comprehensive evaluations of patients’ body and organ functions, 
identifying needs and determining reasonable goals, providing 
appropriate medical interventions to optimize and prepare pa-
tients for the use of specialized ATs, issuing relevant documenta -
tion under regulations or policies, referring to and coordinating 
with appropriate teams for fitting and testing ATs, participating in 
interdisciplinary discussions, monitoring patient usage to identify 
any adverse reactions, and conducting research related to ATs. 
Physicians should also manage the coexisting medical problems to 
facilitate the use of ATs. For instance, a senior with osteoarthritis 
may limit the usage of particular ATs because of the pain. 
 Adequate management and intervention will improve the adher -
ence and acceptance of ATs. Proper training for the user and 
caregivers on how to use and maintain the technology is essential 
• Fig. 20.11 The physician’s role in assistive technology delivery.
for its  effectiv e utilization. Continuous follow-up and feedback 
from the user are necessary to identify any issues, provide ongoing 
support, and make necessary adjustments to the technology 
(Fig. 20.11).
Summary
Advances in AT are transforming the landscape of rehabilitation 
and daily living for individuals with disabilities. As we look to the 
future, the continued integration of emerging technologies, such 
as AI, robotics, and wearable devices, will drive further innovation 
and accessibility. By prioritizing personalization, affordability, and 
interdisciplinary collaboration, the future of ATs promises to en-
hance independence, productivity, and quality of life for individu-
als with disabilities.
